E. A clock solely measures another clock.
F. Cannot infer the answer based on the given information.

B. A clock measures the absolute progression of the universe.

That led to "God is salvation", or Hebrew "Yahshua", which in translation became my proper name, changing to late Greek, "Iesous", Then to late Latin, "Iesus", and finally medieval Latin, "Jesus".

reasoning_steps.inference: John says he called himself John originally.
Then the name was confused with Yochanan, then became Yahshua, then Iesous, then Iesus, then Jesus.
Thus the sequence is John -> Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Iesus -> Jesus. Option A lists John -> Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus, which matches (Iesus is intermediate).

qa.question:
According to John, what is the correct sequence of name changes from his original name to 'Jesus'?
A. Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus -> John
B. John -> Jesus -> Iesous -> Yahshua
C. Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus
D. John -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus
E. John -> Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus
F. Cannot infer the answer based on the given information.
Please provide the option corresponding to the only correct answer, enclosed in parentheses, e.g., (X).
option: A. Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus -> John
B. John -> Jesus -> Iesous -> Yahshua
C. Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus
D. John -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus
E. John -> Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus
F. Cannot infer the answer based on the given information.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John
evidence_dialogues.utterance: I called myself John. I almost always do.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: John
evidence_dialogues.utterance: As tales of the resurrection spread, the name was confused with the Hebrew "Yochanan", meaning "God is gracious". My stay on earth was seen as divine proof of immortality. That led to "God is salvation", or Hebrew "Yahshua", which in translation became my proper name, changing to late Greek, "Iesous", Then to late Latin, "Iesus", and finally medieval Latin, "Jesus".

reasoning_steps.inference: John says he called himself John originally.
Then the name was confused with Yochanan, then became Yahshua, then Iesous, then Iesus, then Jesus.
Thus the sequence is John -> Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Iesus -> Jesus. Option A lists John -> Yochanan -> Yahshua -> Iesous -> Jesus, which matches (Iesus is intermediate).

The most direct reading is her general question and his two other pun examples before the historical one.

session_1.text: John is stacking boxes into his pickup track as his friends arrive.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Hey, buddy! You don't waste time, do you?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: John and Dan lift a heavy box on to the truck.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Would you like to tell us what the hell that was all about?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Kind of the point of a good-bye party, John!

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Went to a certain amount of trouble, you know?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Could've at least stayed a few minutes, huh?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Eaten some of the food we so feverishly prepared?

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: But why are you moving so quickly, you only resigned a couple of days ago!

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: You got the history chair at Stanford.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Well, tacos, chicken wings, tuna salad and beer.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: If we’d had more time, we'd'a done something a little more grandiose.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Art's gonna be along too. He's, uh, talking to a student. Pfft.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Is George taking over for you?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: George or Trimbell - Has the dean made up his mind?

session_1.speaker: Sandy
session_1.text: Edith suddenly notices the painting behind John.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: My god! Wh-what is this? It looks like a van Gogh, but I've never seen it before!

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: No, it's just a gift someone gave me...

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Still, it's a superb copy! Contemporaneous, I think! May I...take a closer look?

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Yeah! It's the same stretcher as van Gogh used.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Yeah, there's writing on the back, it's in French

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Oh, "to my friend Jacque Borne." Wonder who that was?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Harry moves towards the inside, the rest slowly follow.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Surely you'll have this looked at, appraised?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Well, maybe sometime, but I... wouldn't...

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: (taking the painting and putting it in the truck)

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Put that stuff in the kitchen.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: No, I'm going to put it in the bathroom, John.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Gas is off, electricity's on. Get comfortable while you can, furniture's going this afternoon.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Edith and John follow the others inside.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: 'Sbeen years since I sat on a floor.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Heh, I can't remember her name.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Eh, it's good for the back.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: So, you're leaving. Rather suddenly, you must admit.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Truth time, John. Is there a problem?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Oh c'mon, you know we want to help.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: That's appreciated, but really - there's no problem.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Well now, I am curious. Where are you going?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: A decade of professorship, in line to chair the department, and you don't know where you're going?

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Call it cabin fever. After a while, I get itchy feet. I've done this before.

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: No, no, no, you're too young to have done this before.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: And he hasn't aged a day in ten years! Any woman on the faculty would give anything to have that secret.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Is that what they're after, Edith?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Most people can't bag a deer with a rifle and a telescopic sight!

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: The best. Wild game. Lives naturally, eats naturally.

session_1.speaker: Edith
session_1.text: Enter: Art and Linda on Art's bike.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: So, do I get an A for Awesome?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Oh my gosh. That was fun!

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Hey! John! You know Linda. You had her last semester.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: She's one of my victims now; I'm taking her home. She wanted to come by and say hello- goodbye.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Is Art as tough as I hear?

session_1.speaker: Linda
session_1.text: Oh! Archaeology's tough. Dr. Jenkins is a fine teacher.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Something for you to read on the road, pal.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: "Shadows of the cave: parallels to early man."

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I'd rather read than write another one.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: Oh! Everybody, this is Linda. Linda, this is everybody.

session_1.speaker: Art
session_1.text: So! Where you going, John, like we give a damn?

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: We've already covered that. John's got itchy feet.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: There are over-the-counter remedies for that, John.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I just like to move on, now and then.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: I'm sorry I don't have more to offer you.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Got conversation. Some seats for your behinds.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: John lifts his finger as if he's remembered something and suddenly moves for the door.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Is he ducking out on us again?

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Oh ho ho! Johnny Walker Green!

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Ha ha, didn't even know they made it in green!

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: Nothing is too good for my friends.

session_1.speaker: John
session_1.text: But I'm sorry, we're down to plastic cups now.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Ooh! Here, cups, cups? There we go!

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Step on in, there ya go!

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Heh, we're not gonna card you darling!

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Oh, alright, here, join the circle at least!

session_1.speaker: Dan
session_1.text: Well. To long life, and good fortune to our esteemed friend, and colleague John Oldman. May he find undeserved bliss wherever he goes.

session_1.speaker: Harry
session_1.text: Everyone makes themselves comfortable, Linda warming herself in front of the fire.

session_2.text: Scene 4: The Burin

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: John, we're all sorry to see you go. Truly.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Okay. Now we're done with that, what do we do for the rest of the afternoon?

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Anyone got a good topic?

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Dan picks up a carved flint tool from a box.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Like this, maybe? Heh.

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: What is that?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: It's a burin. Of a parrot beak. Inclined chisel point...probably early Magdalenian.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: May I see that?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Sure.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: (hands it over)

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: (examines it carefully)

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Yes indeed, that's what it is.

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: What's a...burin?

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: A burin's a flint tool for grooving wood and bone, antlers especially, to make spear and harpoon points. Magdalenians weren't noted for flint work, so this is a very nice specimen.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: (smirking)

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Okay. What's a "Magdalenian"?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: A later Cro-Magnon, without getting technical.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: It's the final culture of the upper-Paleolithic.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: If stones could speak, eh, Art?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: So where'd you get that, John?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Believe it or not, from a thrift shop. 5 bucks.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: You lucky dog! Me, I gotta go digging for this kinda stuff.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Can I, uh...?

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Yeah.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Huh...

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: (motioning to Edith then handing her the burin)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (surveying the room)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: ...Maybe...

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: I'm glad you did this.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Did what?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: You mean, come over?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: ...Maybe?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Definitely.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Gee, thanks!

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Well, so are we(?).

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: So are we! We couldn't let you just run off.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (taking the burin from Edith to put back into the box)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Thanks.
Scene 5a: The Set-up

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: John, what is up, huh?! Are you running from the cops? We won't turn you in!

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (pacing)

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Yeah come on, out with it. You're among friends.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Snoopy friends.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Forget it.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: You are creating the mystery here. Obviously you have something you'd like to say. Say it!

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Well, maybe I...

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: Harry, stop.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: There is something I'm tempted to tell you, I think. I've never done this before and I wonder how it'll pan out.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: ...I wonder if I could ask you a silly question.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: John we're teachers, we answer silly questions all the time.

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: Hey!

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: (elbowing Art)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: What if a man from the upper Paleolithic...survived until the present day?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: ...Wh-what d'ya mean "survived"? Never died?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Yes. What would he be like?

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Arnold Schwarzenegger.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: A few smirks.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: It's an interesting idea. What, are you working on a science-fiction story?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Say I am. What would he be like?

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Pretty tired.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Everyone chuckles.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Well, seriously, as Art's book title suggests, he might be like any of us.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Dan! A caveman?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Well, there's no anatomical difference between, say, a Cro-Magnon and us.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Except that as a rule, we've grown taller.

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: What's the selective advantage of height?

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Better to see predators in tall grass, my dear.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Actually, tall and skinny radiates heat more effectively in warmer climates.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: And as for Neanderthals, that strain's still with us, of course.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Arnold Schwarzenegger!

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: But...he'd be a caveman.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: No, he wouldn't. John's hypothetical man would've lived through a hundred and forty centuries...

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Yeah, roughly.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: ...And changed with every one of them. I mean, assuming normal intelligence. Well, we think men of the upper Paleolithic were as intelligent as we are. They just didn't know as much.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: John's man would have learned as the race learned. In fact, if he had an inquiring mind, his knowledge might be...astonishing.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: (pointing at John)

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: If you do write that, let me have a look at it.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: I'm sure you'll make some anthropological boners.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: It's a deal.

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: What would keep him alive?

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: What does the biologist say?

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Cigarettes. And ice-cream.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Everyone scoffs and chuckles.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Alright, alright, I'll play, I'll play.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Alright, um, in science fiction terms, I would say...

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: ...Perfect cellular regeneration caused by circulating Mesenchymal stem cells originating from bone marrow. Especially in the vital organs. Actually, the human body appears designed to live about a hundred and ninety years. Most of us just die...

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: (motions to his glass)

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: ...of slow poisoning.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: (smiles and takes a sip)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Maybe he did something right. Something everybody else in history had done wrong.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: What, like eat the food, drink the water and breathe the air?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: ...Prior to modern times, those were pristine.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: We've extended our lifespan in a world that's, uh... not fit to live in.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: (sighs)

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Y'know, it could happen! Yeah! The pancreas turns over cells every twenty-four hours, the stomach lining in three days, the entire body in seven years, but the process falters. Waste accumulates, eventually proves fatal to function. Now if a quirk in his immune system led to perfect detox, perfect renewal, then yeah. He could, uh... duck decay.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Mm, that's a secret we'd all love to have.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Would you really want to do that? ...Live 14,000 years?

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Well, if I could stay healthy and I didn't age, I mean, why not?

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: Yeah. What a chance to learn.

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: Is anyone hungry?

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: (Heads off-stage to the kitchen)
Scene 5b: The Revelation

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Y'know, the more I think about it, yeah, it's possible. Anything is possible, right? After all, one century's magic, another century's science. They thought Columbus was a nut-case, right? Pasteur? Copernicus?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Aristarchus long before that.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (Nonchalantly, as he gets up to pour himself another drink)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: I had a chance to sail with Columbus, only I'm not the adventurous type.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: What?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Music starts...

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: I was pretty sure the Earth was round, but at that point, I still thought he might fall off an edge some place...

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Incredulous, confused reactions.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: (Bemused, laughing nervously)

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Look around, John. We just did.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: I suppose there's a joke in there somewhere, but I don't get it.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: There's nothing to get.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Music builds...

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: What are we talking about?

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: We were just talking about a caveman...who survives until the present time.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: As you said, what a chance to learn, once I learned to learn.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Did you start the whiskey before we got here?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Pretend it's science fiction. Figure it out.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Okay, a- (heh heh) -very old Cro-Magnon living until the- present.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Music crescendos...

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (slowly leans forward and suddenly GRUNTS LOUDLY like a caveman)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Everyone jumps, startled, and then laughs.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Oh!
Scene 6a: The First Lifetime

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: (entering)

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: What?

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: John just confided that he's fourteen thousand years old.

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: Oh, John, you don't look a day over nine hundred!

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: (laughing)

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Okay!

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Alright Spock, I'll play your little game. What do you want? What's the punch line?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Every ten years or so, when people start to notice I don't age, I move on.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Pause.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: ...That's very good, that's very quick John. I wanna read that story when you're done.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: You want more?

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Yeah, by all means. This is great! Alright, now... (chuckles) ...So you think you're a- a- uh- Cro-Magnon.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Well, I didn't learn it in school. That's my best guess. Based on archaeological data, maps, anthropological research. Since Mesopotamia, I've got the last...four thousand years straight.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Well you're ahead of most people, so please, go on.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Well you know the background stuff, so I'll make it brief. In what I call my first lifetime, I aged to about thirty-five. What you see. I ended up leading my group. They saw me as magical. I didn't even have to fight for it.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Then, fear came. And they chased me away. They thought that I was...stealing their lives away to stay young.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: The prehistoric origin of the vampire myth. That is good!

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: First thousand years, I didn't know up from sideways.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: How do you know the first thousand years?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: An informed guess, based on what I've learned in my memories.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Most people can scarcely remember their childhood, but you have memories of that time?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Like yours. Selective. Y'know, the high points, the low points, traumas. They stick in the mind forever. Put down at three or thirty-five, you still feel a twinge.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Go on.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: I kept getting chased because I wouldn't die, so I got the hang of joining new groups I found. I also got the idea of periodically moving on. We were semi-nomadic, of course, following the weather and the game we hunted. The first two- thousand years were cold. We learned it was warmer at lower elevations. Late glacial period, I assume.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: What was the terrain like?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Mountainous. Vast plains to the West-

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: West - something you learned in school.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Towards the setting sun. I suspect I saw the British Isles from what is now the French coast.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Huge mountains on the other side of an enormous deep valley that was shadowed by the setting sun. This is before they were separated from the continent by rising seas, as the glaciers melted.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: That happened?

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Yes, the end of the Pleistocene. So far what he says fits.

session_2.speaker: Art
session_2.text: Oh, yeah, into any textbook.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: And that's where I found it. How can I have knowledgeable recall if I didn't have knowledge?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: It's all retrospective. All I can do is integrate my recollections with modern findings.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Caveman, you gonna hit me over the head with a club and drag me into the bedroom?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: You'd be more fun conscious.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Oh, John.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Let me get this straight. We're not talking about, uh, reincarnation. You're not saying that you remember, whatever the hell it would be, 200 separate lifetimes, dying and being born again and yada yada?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: One lifetime.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Some lifetime.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: (laughs)

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Wow! Maybe there is something to this reincarnation thing. You're supposed to come back again and again, learn and learn, and somehow, John, you just managed to bypass all the other bodies.

session_2.speaker: Dan
session_2.text: Well, what's the point?

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: What about oceans?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Didn't see them till much later.

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: So how would you know an ocean from a lake?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Big waves. Something else I can only surmise in retrospect.

session_2.speaker: Linda
session_2.text: Were you curious about where it all came from?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: We would look up at the sky and wonder. "There's gotta be some big guys up there. What else made all this down here?"

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: At first I thought there was, uh, something wrong with me - maybe I was a bad guy for not dying. Then I began to wonder if I was cursed, or perhaps blessed. Then I thought maybe I had a mission.

session_2.speaker: Edith
session_2.text: Do you still think you do? God works in mysterious ways.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: I think I just happened.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Phone rings, aside.

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: (laughs)

session_2.speaker: Harry
session_2.text: Wow.
Scene 6b: Ellie's Midterm

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (picking up the phone, set slightly away from stage)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Hello?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Yes, Ellie?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: What's wrong?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (calls out)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Sandy?

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: Coming.

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: Yeah?

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Do we have Ellie's midterm here?

session_2.speaker: Sandy
session_2.text: Yeah, sorry. I picked it up with the periodicals.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Got it.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: No, you're worried about your parents? Don't- don't worry. You passed, C+.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: (Changes grade on paper)

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Take care of yourself.

session_2.speaker: John
session_2.text: Good kid. What does a pre-Med need with history?

session_3.text: Scene 6c: Explanations

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: Sorry, guys. John, please continue.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Come on, I thought we were done with that.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: No! Let's go on with it. It's interesting.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Besides, I think he's making a certain amount of sense.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Like Hegel: logic from absurd premises.

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: That Van Gogh?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: He gave it to me. I was, uh, Jacque Borne at the time.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: A pig farmer.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: A pig farmer?

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Harry and Art Laugh, the ladies giggle/smile.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I like to work with my hands. He would come out to the place, paint. We talked about capturing nature in Art. Turner, Cezanne, Pissarro.

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: Oh, the Nolde landscapes.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Not in Van Gogh's time. He would have loved them, though.

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: Yes.

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Well, I don't understand why you can't remember where you're from. Geography hasn't changed. I learned that in-

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Professor Hansen’s tepid lectures. But you're right.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Where did you live when you were five years old?

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Little Rock.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Your mother, she took you to the market?

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Mm-hmm.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: What direction was it? From your house.

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: I-I don't know.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: How far?

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Um, three blocks.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Were there any references that stuck in your mind?

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Well, there was a gas station and a big field. I was told I could never go there alone.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: And if you went back there today, would it be the same?

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: No. I'm sure it's all different and built up.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Thus the saying: "You can't go home again."

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Because it isn't there anymore. Picture it on my scale- I migrated through an endless flat space full of endless new things- Forests, mountains, tundra, canyons. My memory sees what I saw then.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: My eye sees highways, flyovers, urban sprawl, Big Macs under the Eiffel tower.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Early on, the world got bigger and bigger, and then...

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Think what I've had to unlearn.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: And now you're moving on.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: As you've said, there's talk of my not aging, and when that happens, I move on.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Well, it might make sense to set up your next identity, your next ten years, and then just drop into it.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I've done that a few times, even passed as my own son. "Oh, you're an engineer, too? You're Ben's son. He was a good man." Saves trouble with credentials and references.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: On the other hand, I've been busted a few times.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Spent a year in jail, Belgium, 1862- I won't forget that- For faking a government application.

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: When'd you come to America?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: 1890, right after Van Gogh's death, with some French immigrants...

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Moving On.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Pause.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: An answer for every question. Except one, John.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Why're you doing this?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: A whim. Maybe not such a good idea. I... wanted to say goodbye to you as me, not what you thought I was.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Well, since this isn't funny, we think you might have a problem. A very serious problem.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I've got boxes to move.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: I'll give you a hand.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Wouldn't you have some relic, an artifact to remind you of your early life? Like this, maybe?

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: (holds up the burin)

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Thrift shop. Really.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: If you lived a hundred- a thousand years...

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: (holding up a pen)

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Would you still have this? What would cause you to keep it? As a memento to your beginnings, even if you didn't have the concept of beginnings? It would be gone, lost.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: No. I don't have artifacts.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: (tosses the pen to Harry)

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: (to Dan)

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Keep that.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Interesting. You could have lied about that.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Don't talk about me while I'm gone.
Scene 7: Sandy
John and Sandy load a few boxes. Sandy pauses

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: I love you, you know.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: Soft music.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I know.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: Since my first week at the office.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: ...And?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I care very much about you, but now you know what you'd be getting into.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: Do you really think you're a caveman?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Do you?

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: Could you love me, or don't you believe in that anymore?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I've gotten over it too many times.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Fond of you... Certainly attracted to you.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Sandy giggles.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: That's it?

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: ...I can work with that.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: If what I'm saying is true, you and any children will age. I won't. And one day I'll leave.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: You'll go back to your May-December romances.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: The simple fact is that I can't give you forever.

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: How long's forever? Who ever really has it?

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: My parents split up before I was born, and then my mom's next marriage lasted what, a whole three years?

session_3.speaker: Sandy
session_3.text: Then there's death, illness, acts of god...no one knows how long they have. Or how little. I love you. Take whatever you can get.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Like ten years?
Scene 8: Cave-paintings and Buddha

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Is he serious?

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Art walks over to the phone.

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: If he is, I'm sorry to say he's...

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: Oh, how could he have concealed that for ten years?

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: At least he doesn't appear to be dangerous.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: What are you doing?

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Checkin' for a hidden mic. Candid Camera.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Art is on the phone to one side.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: He's fabricating these wild stories. I've never seen him acting like this.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Oh, it's crazy.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Alright. Alright, a-as soon as you can, then.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: As John enters the room, Harry jumps him, yelling.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Ah! Ha ha ha!

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: John expertly counters, and pushes him to the floor.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Uhn! Jeez! Oh.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Why did you do that?

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: I wanted to see how fast you were. Check your reflexes.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I don't have eyes in the back of my head, I can't hear a flea walking, I am not in any way superman.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Yeah. Well, I'm a second-degree black belt.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Give it another thousand years.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: (Gives him a hand)

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Well. I got it, I got it, I got it.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: (Struggling to his feet)

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Oh. Jesus.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Smooth demonstration, Harry.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: Sit on it, Dan.

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: I still have questions.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Ya, I-I do too, John. I mean, a-a-are we done with prehistory yet? Remember any of your original language?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: A little. One thing hasn't changed much...

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: (wolf whistles towards Sandy, causing her to blush)

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Did you ever do any cave Art?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Do you know the rock Art at Les Eyzies?

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Mm-hmm?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: It was the work of a man named... Giraud. He did a pretty good job. He would draw the animals that we hoped to find to eat. One day after a fruitless hunt, our chief stomped his teeth out because his magic had failed him. After that, someone had to chew his food for him. Finally, he got- I suspect- An infected jaw, and he was abandoned.

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: That's awful.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: You have to know what to kill.

session_3.speaker: Linda
session_3.text: Is this why all your students say your knowledge of history is...so amazing?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: No, that's mostly based on study. Remember, it's one man, one place at a time, my solitary viewpoint of a world I knew almost nothing about.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Well, let's talk about what you say you do know about- Historical times.

session_3.speaker: Edith
session_3.text: Don't encourage him.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: Edith.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Next few thousand years, it got warmer.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: A few thousand years- See, now, I know you're guessing.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: You can't get there from here, Art.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Well then, pray, continue.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: We hunted reindeer, mammoths-

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: ...Bison, horses. The game retreated northward as the climate changed, you got the idea of growing food rather than gathering it, raising animals rather than hunting them. Am- Am I- Am I getting warm, here? I bet I am. Lakeside living becomes commonplace! Fishing, fowling- Come on!

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: John, this is out of any textbook.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Even yours. You got most of it right. Eventually I headed to the East. I'd grown curious about the world. I'd gotten the hang of going it alone, learning how to fit in when I wanted to.

session_3.speaker: Dan
session_3.text: East. Towards the rising sun?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Yes. I thought it might be warmer there. That's when I saw an ocean. The Mediterranean, probably. It was around the beginning of the Bronze Age, so I followed the trade routes from the East. Copper. Tin. Learning languages as I went. Everywhere, creation myths, new gods, so many, so different. I finally realized that it was...probably all hogwash.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: So I was Sumerian for 2,000 years, then finally Babylonian under Hammurabi. Great man. And I sailed as a Phoenician for a time.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: See, moving on had been easier as a Hunter- Gatherer...difficult when villages emerged.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Tougher still in city states where authority was centralized. Strangers were suspect. It seemed as though I was always moving on.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I learned some new tricks- even faked my death a couple of times.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: I continued east to India, luckily at the time of Gautama Buddha.

session_3.speaker: Art
session_3.text: Luckily.

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Most extraordinary man I've ever known. He taught me things I'd never thought about before.

session_3.speaker: Harry
session_3.text: You studied... with the Buddha?

session_3.speaker: John
session_3.text: Until he died. He knew there was something different about me. I never told him.

session_4.text: Scene 9a: Dr. Gruber

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: This is fascinating. I almost wish it were true.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Y-y-y-eah, if it was true, why are you telling us? I mean, we might leave here today, we'll go out there, tell everybody!

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: It would vanish in disbelief. A story that goes around the room. No credibility. Even if I could make you believe me, in a month, you wouldn't.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Some of you would call me a psychopath, others would be angry at a pointless joke.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Well John, Some of us are angry now.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: This- This was a bad idea. Uh, I love you all, and I do not want to put you through anything.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Then why are you doing it?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: 'Cause I wanted to say goodbye-

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: ...As yourself. I think you've done that, whoever that self is.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Easy, Edith. We're just grading his homework.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: I see what's going on. You're playing the good cop, Dan. That's fine. Just enjoy it.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: All right, I think this whole thing is just a crock! I should leave, but I'm gonna stay. You know why? 'Cause I wanna see what this is all about.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: So do I. What is this all about?

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Well, let's ask Dr. Freud, who's just arrived.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Hey, Will! Will!

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Art. Hey.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: John! I'm glad I caught you. Someone mentioned that you were leaving today-

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Called you, told you that I've lost it. Glad you're here. Things are going in unexpected directions.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Yes, so I hear.

session_4.speaker: Sandy
session_4.text: Hi. Are you hungry?

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Uh, thank you, no.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: Whiskey? Johnny Walker green!

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Ohhh, yes!

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: (he chaperones the party to the seating)

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: You look very familiar, my dear.

session_4.speaker: Linda
session_4.text: Linda Murphy. I'm in your Tuesday Psych 1 class, Dr. Gruber.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Ah, well, this lesson may be something I could not have imagined.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: I regret being so...obvious about this, John, but these people are all very concerned for you.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Yes, I'm cutting out paper dinosaurs.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: I really wish I'd been here from the beginning.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Me too.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Well, let me just say something right now.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: There's absolutely no way in the whole world for John to prove this story to us, just like there's no way for us to disprove it. No matter how outrageous we think it is, no matter how highly trained some of us think we are, there's absolutely no way to disprove it. Our friend is either a caveman, a liar, or a nut. So while we're thinking about that, why don't we just go with it? I mean, hell, who knows, he might jolt us into believing him, or we might jolt him back to reality.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Believing?!

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Whose reality?

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: So... You're a caveman.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Yes. Uh... Uh, I was a Cro-Magnon, I think.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: You don't know if you're a caveman or not?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: No, I'm sure about that.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: A Cro-Magnon, then. When did you first realize this?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Well, when the Cro-Magnon was first identified, when anthropology gave them a name, I had mine.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Well, please continue. I'm sure you must have more to say.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Would you like for me to lie on the couch, doctor?

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: (laughing, patting the couch)

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: As you wish.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: As a physician, I'm curious. In this enormous lifetime you describe, have you ever been ill?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Sure, as much as anyone.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Seriously ill?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Sometimes.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Of what? Do you know?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: In prehistory, I can't tell you. Maybe pneumonia once or twice. Last few hundred years, I've gotten over typhoid, yellow fever, smallpox...I survived the black plague.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Bubonic? Oh, that's terrible.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: More so than history describes.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: And smallpox- But you're not scarred.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I don't scar.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: No, John, that is not possible.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Please, let's take John's story at face value and explore it from that perspective. If he doesn't scar, it's no stranger than the rest.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: John, would you please stop by my lab before you take off, suffer a few tests from your friendly neighborhood biologist?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I'm leery of labs. Afraid I might go in and stay for a thousand years while cigarette-smoking men try to figure me out.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: You don't think that I would betray you in any way?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Walls have ears.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Medical tests might be a way of proving what you say.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I don't wanna prove it.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: So you're telling us this, the yarn of the century, and you don't care if we believe it or not?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I guess I shouldn't have expected you to. You're not as crazy as you think I am.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Amen.

session_4.speaker: Sandy
session_4.text: I've always liked you.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Why, thank you, dear.

session_4.speaker: Sandy
session_4.text: Now that's changing.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Surely you don't believe this nonsense?

session_4.speaker: Sandy
session_4.text: I think we should remain courteous to someone who we've known and trusted, Edith.

session_4.speaker: Linda
session_4.text: Here you sit- You can't break his story. All you can do is thumb your nose at it.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Is that what you're doing, John? Are you laughing at us inside?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I wish you didn't feel that way.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: What you're saying- It offends common sense!

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: So does relativity, quantum mechanics- That's the way nature works.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: But your story doesn't fit into nature as we know it.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: But we know so little, Dan. We know so little. I mean how many of you know five geniuses in your field that you disagree with... One you would like to strangle?

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Ugh, strangle them all.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Damn it, Dan, it's bad enough we have to listen to Harry's idiotic jokes.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: Thank you very much, Edith. Maybe when I'm 110, I'll be as smart as you are.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: If you lived as long as John did, you still wouldn't grow up.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Come on, guys. Take it easy. How often do we get to meet someone who says he's a stone-age man?

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: (mutters)

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: Well, once is enough.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: Edith.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: All right. A guy with your mind- You'd have studied a great deal.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I have ten degrees, including all of yours...

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Except yours, Will.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: That makes me feel a trifle Lilliputian.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: That's over the span of 170 years. I got my biology degree at Oxford in 1840, so I'm a little behind the times. The same in other areas- I can't keep up with the new stuff that comes along. No one can. Not even in their specialty.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: So much for the myth of the super-wise, all- knowing immortal.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: I see your point, John. No matter how long a man lives, he can't be in advance of his times. He can't know more than the best of the race knows, if that- I mean, when the world learned it was round, you learned it.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: It took some time. News traveled slowly before communications were fancy. There were social obstacles, preconceptions, screams from the church.

session_4.speaker: Art
session_4.text: Ten doctorates. That's impressive, John. Did you teach them?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Some. You might have all done the same. Living 14,000 years didn't make me a genius. I just had time.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Time. We can't see it, we can't hear it, we can't weigh it, we can't measure it in a laboratory. It's a subjective sense of becoming what we are instead of what we were a nanosecond ago, becoming what we will be in another nanosecond.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: The Hopis see time as a landscape, existing before and behind us, and we move- We move through it, slice by slice.

session_4.speaker: Linda
session_4.text: Clocks measure time.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: No, they measure themselves. The objective referent of a clock is another clock.

session_4.speaker: Edith
session_4.text: How very interesting. What has it got to do with John?

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Oh, he- He might be a man who lives outside of time as we know it.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Yes, uh, well. People do go around armed these days.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: (Points at John with hand in jacket pocket, as if holding a gun)

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: If I shot you, John- You're immortal? Would you survive this?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: I never said I was immortal, just old. I might die. And then you could wonder the rest of your incarcerated life what you shot.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Well, uh, may I?

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: (Pulls out a pipe)

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Everyone sighs with relief.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: Preferable to a gun.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Will, that was a bit much.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: (peering into a box)

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Ooh. Books. Doctorates. Yes, you have grown and changed. But there is always innate nature.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Wouldn't you be more comfortable squatting in the backyard?

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Sometimes I do, will. Look up at the stars.

session_4.speaker: Dan
session_4.text: Wonder.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: And what did primitive man make of them?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: A great mystery. There were gods up there then.

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: Shamans who knew about them told us.

session_4.speaker: Harry
session_4.text: They still do.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Have you ever wished it would end?

session_4.speaker: John
session_4.text: No.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Fourteen thousand years. Injuries, illness, disasters. You've survived them all. You're a very lucky man.

session_4.speaker: Will
session_4.text: Pensive pause, followed by a knock at the door.

session_5.text: Scene 9b: CharityNow and Death

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Come in.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #1 John Oldman?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Yes.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #1 CharityNow. We're here to pick up the furniture.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: It's all yours.

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: Here, take this chair. I'm gonna go drink in the corner.

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: CharityNow guys start removing the furniture.

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: You're, uh... you're donating it? Everything?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I'll get more.

session_5.speaker: Edith
session_5.text: Do you always travel this light?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: It's the only way to move.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Oh, you- You've talked a good deal about your extraordinary amount of living. What do you think of dying, John? Do you fear death?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Who wouldn't?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: How did primitive man regard death?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Well, we had the practical concept. You know, we stopped, fell down, didn't get up, started to smell bad, come apart.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Injuries we could understand- If someone's insides were all over the ground.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Infections... They were, uh, mysterious.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Aging... The biggest mystery of all.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: You realized you were different.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Uh, longer to realize how I was different, to find a way to synthesize my experience into a view of myself. At first, I thought everybody had something wrong with them. They got old and they died, animals, too... but not me.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: You live simply.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I've owned castles, but why leave a lot if you're always leaving? I have money.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: As one grows older, the days, weeks, months go by more quickly. What does a day or a year or a century mean to you? The birth-death cycle?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Turbulence. I meet someone, learn their name, say a word, they're gone. Others come like waves. Rise, fall. Ripples in a wheat field, blown by the wind.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Do you ever get tired of it all?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I get bored now and then. They keep making the same stupid mistakes over and over.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Hey! Then you see yourself as separate from the rest of humanity.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I didn't mean it that way. But of course...I am.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (Chuckling darkly)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Are you comfortable knowing that you have lived while everyone you knew- Everyone you knew, John!- Has died?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I've regretted losing people...often.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Have you ever felt guilt about that- Something akin to survivor's guilt?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: In the strict psychological sense? I suppose I have. Yeah. …But what can I do about it?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Indeed.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #2 (To Edith on the couch)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: I'm sorry, ma'am.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Edith, Dan and Sandy rise off the couch.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Gentlemen, I'm- I'm gonna keep the couch.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Thanks.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Ladies? Will?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Oh, no...

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: You've got a heart condition. Don't grump about it.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (scoffs, defeatedly)

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: Hey, how about changing the subject, Will?

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: Enough with the, uh- With the dying.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: But this is the flip side of this coin, Harry.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: I'm very curious to know his feelings. Would you prefer I asked him about his father?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I thought you always started with "tell me about your mother."

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Yes, but prehistory was strongly patriarchal.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Surely you remember your father?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I seem to remember a figure, perhaps an older brother, a social father, maybe.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Well, no matter. I can scarcely remember mine.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Do you feel a vacancy in your life about that, John, something you wish could be filled by a face, a voice, an image?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Not at this late date.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: There must be someone- Probably many- That you valued intensely?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (wearily)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Loved. You saw them age and die- A friend, a colleague, a- (hesitates, lump in throat)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: ...wife. Certainly you've had wives and children?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I'd move on. I had to move on.

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: Making him history's biggest bigamist.

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: (Chuckles softly)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Have you ever in your life thought, "It should have been me"?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Maybe.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Yeah, Art has told me that some of your early fellows feared you were stealing their lives? Have you thought that perhaps you were?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Perhaps you are! There have always been legends of such a thing, a creature, not quite human taking not the blood, but the life force itself?

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: My god, Will.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (continuing)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: ...Unconsciously, perhaps, by some biological or psychic mechanism that we can only guess at? I'm not saying you would do such a thing deliberately. I'm not saying that you would even know how to...

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: ...Would you?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: ...But would such a thing be fair?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: So you believe me now?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: I'm only exploring what you have said. Whether I believe it or not is of no importance. We will die... You will live. Will you come to my funeral, John?

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: Hey, Will...

session_5.speaker: Sandy
session_5.text: You've gone too far. John didn't ask to be what he is.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: And we did not ask to hear about it. But if it were true, is there one among us who would not feel envy, even perhaps a touch of hatred? You told us of yourself, John. Can you imagine how we feel?

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: I never thought of that.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Since you may not die, while we most assuredly will... there must be a reason for that, no?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Perhaps you are an expert.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: CHARITYNOW GUY #1 Uh, that's it, Mr. Oldman. Have a good one.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Thank you. You too.

session_5.speaker: John
session_5.text: Music gets dark

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Or are you a vampire, John? Even an unknowing one? Do you stand alive and tall in a graveyard that you helped to fill?

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: That's going too far.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (dismissing Dan and continuing with gusto)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Bored, perhaps lonely, because your heart cannot keep its treasures. Is that what you're doing?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Have you led a wrongful life?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Well, then, perhaps...

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (reaching into coat pocket)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: …it is time to die.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (pulls out a gun)

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: Wait a minute, now. Look, I don't know what John is doing, but I sure as hell don't like what you're doing.

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: Now you give me that gun or I'm gonna break your goddamn arm.

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: (John reaches out an arm holding Dan back)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (scornfully)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: You sound like our football coach, Dan.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: What do you think, John? A shot to the arm?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Perhaps we could watch it heal.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: A bullet in the head- What exactly will happen?

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: Dramatic pause...

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (putting down the gun with a sigh)

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: I have papers to correct. As much as I dislike that job, it'll be preferable to this. I leave you with it.

session_5.speaker: Will
session_5.text: (storms off)

session_5.speaker: Dan
session_5.text: Jesus Christ. What the hell was that all about?

session_5.speaker: Edith
session_5.text: Where'd he get a gun?

session_5.speaker: Art
session_5.text: He had you on the ropes, John. Are you really so damn smart?

session_5.speaker: Edith
session_5.text: It's not like Will.

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: (quietly)

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: Mary passed away yesterday.

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: John glances up and dashes out of the door after Will.

session_5.speaker: Linda
session_5.text: Who?

session_5.speaker: Harry
session_5.text: His wife. She had, uh, pancreatic cancer.

session_6.text: Scene 10: Jesus

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Will! I didn't know about Mary. I'm sorry. I can see how this might have hit you.

session_6.speaker: Will
session_6.text: Please, permit me to be infantile by myself.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (holding out his hand, with the other hand on Will's arm)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Will, please.

session_6.speaker: Will
session_6.text: (hands him the gun, turns around and walks away)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (checks gun to find it unloaded)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Meanwhile, inside.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: What the hell were you thinking, Art?

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Oh, come on. Something had to be done.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: I have to say I agree.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: And he's our friend. Whatever else on earth is going on, he's our friend!

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: You sure about that?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Why the hell are you being so hard on him, Edith?

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: One of my favorite people has disappeared. Can you get Alzheimer's at 35? Maybe I'm trying to wake him up. Maybe I'm too sad to cry.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (entering)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: What I said about myself hurt him. He struck back.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Expertly- That stuff about stealing life forces?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: I've always wondered about the reasons.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Well, we still have an afternoon to kill, right?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Charades?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (chuckling)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: No. John? I have a charade, and it is just for you. A'right? Sandy, come here. Come on, come on, come on. Okay, this one's for you.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: A'right. Ready?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (grunts like ape)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (notices Sandy and wolf whistles)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (more grunting, play-acts whacking Sandy on head with club)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Ooh! ...Unh.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (puts arm around Sandy)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: My first wedding?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: There you go. There you go! Very good, and I bet at least one of us...

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (points around the room)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: ...is your direct descendent.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: And I didn't even send a Christmas card.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Christmas card? What about a birthday card? And don't even get me started on the candles... with the blowing and the...

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (huffs)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: ...and all the candles!

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Yeah, all right. I tried.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Well, uh, call me underdeveloped, but I'd like to hear more.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Me too.

session_6.speaker: Linda
session_6.text: More.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: You double-damn swear this isn't some cockamamie science fiction story you're pulling on us?

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Next question.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: You- You- You realize this is an invitation to men in white suits with happy pills.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Think about it- A mechanism allowing survival for thousands of years?

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Run out of room even faster.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Well then we'd have to go to Mars as a colony as we expanded, as we'd have to.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: I'd like that. On a planet of another star?

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: I envy you.

session_6.speaker: Linda
session_6.text: Did you have a pet dinosaur?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (smiling)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: They were a little bit before my time.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: At least something is.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: No doubt you could give us a thousand details, John, corroborating your story, from the Magdalene to the Buddha to now.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Ten thousand, and you could stay out of the books.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: Oh, it's getting chilly.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (beckoning to the fireplace)

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Here, come over here. Join me.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: That, uh, raises an interesting question, John.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Could there be others like you who escaped the aging process as you have?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Representing something terrific we don't even know about biology.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: We're learning all the time.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Yeah, but how would he know? He doesn't wear an armband, an I.D. Badge saying "yabba dabba doo."

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: (chuckles)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: There was a man in the 1600s.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: (interrupting)

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: Where were you in 1292 A.D.?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Where were you a year ago on this date?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Anyway, it was the 1600s, and I met a man, and I had a hunch that he was...like me, so I told him.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Ah. See, you said this was a first.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: I forgot.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: A crack in your story, John?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: A touch of senility.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Anyway, he said yes, but from another time, another place. We talked for two days. It was all pretty convincing, but we couldn't be sure.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: We each confirmed what the other said, but how do we know if the confirmation was genuine or an echo? I knew I was legit, but I thought, "maybe he's playing a game on me." You know, a scholar of all we spoke about. He said he was inclined with the same reservation.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Now, that's interesting. Just as we can never be sure, even if we wanted to- I mean, if we were sure, you couldn't be sure of that.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: We parted, agreeing to keep in touch- But of course, we didn't- And 200 years later I thought I saw him in a train station in Brussels. Lost him in the crowd.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: Oh, what a shame.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: I- I mean, if it were true.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Okay, here's one for you. What do you do in your spare time?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (laughing)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Oh every 50 years or so, when I wanna get away from the rush, I go down to a primitive tribe in New Guinea, Where I'm worshipped as an immortal god, and they have a huge statue of me. It's a big party. Yeah, I've got a lot of pictures of it, but I've already packed them up. I'm sorry.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: I won't make the obvious nasty crack about more unwashed cavemen.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Actually, bathing was the style until the middle ages when the church told us it was sinful to wash away god's dirt, so people were sewn into their underwear in October and they popped out in April.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: You said you just happened. I don't believe that. If your story's true, why did God allow you to happen?

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: That makes an interesting point. Are you religious, John?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: I don't follow a known religion. No.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Ever?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Long time ago I did, like most people. Some just never get over it.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Do you believe in god?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: As Laplace said, "I have no need of that hypothesis." He may be around, though.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: He's everywhere. We just can't see him.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Pfft. If this were the best I could do, I'd be hiding, too.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: And creation...

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: It's here, I'm not so sure it was created.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: What then?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Maybe it's just accumulated, fields affecting fields.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: What about the source of the field energies?

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Wouldn't that imply a prime mover?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: I'd wonder about the source of the prime mover, and turtles all the way down, infinite regress, but that doesn't imply anything to me.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: ...Back to the mystery.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: It's a very old question, but there's no answer except in religious terms. If you have faith, it's answered.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Did you ever meet any person from our religious history? A Biblical figure?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: In a way.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: Who?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: We should skip this one.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: No, no, no skipping. Come on.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Next question.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: No, come on!

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Everyone clamoring.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Come on, spit it out!

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Good lord! You were one of them!

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: This is going in a direction that I- I- I- I didn't expect. I hoped it wouldn't- We...should call it a night.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Come on! You were someone in religious history?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (hesitates)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Yes.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: In the bible?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: (hesitates)

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Yes.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Someone we know?

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: How could we not know someone in the bible?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: I mean somebody important.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: You may think you know him, but it's mostly myth.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: (loudly)

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: The entire bible is...mostly m-myth and allegory with maybe some...basis in historical events.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Y-You...were part of that history?!

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Yes.

session_6.speaker: Linda
session_6.text: Moses.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Moses was based on Misis, a Syrian myth, and there are earlier versions- All found floating on water, the staff that changed to a snake, waters that were parted so followers could be led to freedom and even receive laws on stone or wooden tablets.

session_6.speaker: Linda
session_6.text: One of the apostles

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: They weren't really apostles. They didn't do any real teaching. Peter the fisherman learned a little more about fishing.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: How do you know that?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: The mythical overlay is so enormous...and not good. The truth is so… so… simple.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: The New Testament in a hundred words or less.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: You ready?

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: I don't think I wanna hear this. Harry, will you take me home?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: No! Not right now. I do want to hear this.

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Sit down, Edith. You act like you believe him.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: (softly)

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: It's sacrilege.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: How can it be sacrilege? He hasn't said anything yet.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: (righteously)

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: The new New Testament is sacrilege.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: There are a dozen new new testaments, from Hebrew to Greek to Latin to Tyndale, all the way to King James, all revisionist, and all called revealed truth.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: I mean a new New Testament in 100 words.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: I can give you the Ten Commandments in ten words: Don't. Don't, don't, don't. Don't, don't, don't, don't, don't, don't.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: (laughing)

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Don't. The commandments are just modern updates of more ancient laws. Hammurabi's code.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: That's right, and they weren't the first, right?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Edith, I was raised on the Torah... My wife, on the Quran. My oldest son is an Atheist. My youngest is a Scientologist. My daughter is studying Hinduism. I imagine that there is room there for a holy war in my living room. But we practice live and let live.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Why don't you sit down?

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Edith hesitantly sits

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: What is your preferred version of the bible?

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: The King James, of course. It's the most modern, the work of great scholars.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: Modern is good.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Alright, John, hit us with the short form.

session_6.speaker: Harry
session_6.text: Choral/religious music...

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Guy met the Buddha, likes what he heard, thought about it for a while- Say 500 years, while he returned to the Mediterranean, became an Etruscan. Seeped into the Roman Empire. He didn't like what they became- A giant killing machine. He went to the near East thinking, "Why not pass the Buddha's teachings on in a modern form?" So he tried. One dissident against Rome?

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Rome won. The rest is history. Well, sort of.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Lot of fairy tales mixed in.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: (quietly)

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: I knew it.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: He's saying he was Christ.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Oh, no. That's the medal they pinned on Jesus to fulfill prophecy.

session_6.speaker: Dan
session_6.text: The crucifixion.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: He blocked the pain, as he had learned to do in Tibet and India. He also learned to slow his body processes down to the point where they were undetectable. They thought he was dead. So his followers pulled him from the cross, placed him in a cave... His body normalized as he had trained it to... He attempted to go away undetected, but some devotees were standing watch. Tried to explain. They were ecstatic.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: Thus, I was resurrected, and I ascended to central Europe to get away as far as possible.

session_6.speaker: Edith
session_6.text: You don't mean a word of this, John. My god, why are you doing this?!

session_6.speaker: Art
session_6.text: Let me see your wrists.

session_6.speaker: John
session_6.text: I don't scar. Besides, they tied me...but nails and blood make better religious Art.
